Analyst Note – www.carbontracker.org 4 Paris Maligned III 3 Introduction The worsening impacts of climate change become more evident with each passing year, as temperature records are broken, wildfires blaze, and droughts wreak havoc on food systems. As the 10th anniversary of the Paris Agreement approaches, the goals enshrined in it are quickly slipping out of reach, with the 1.5˚C temperature threshold at risk of being irreversibly breached. While demand for fossil fuels – the primary driver of rising emissions – is on the verge of peaking as the energy transition progresses, the world is still heading for 2.4-3.1˚C of warming, well above what scientists and governments have deemed safe.1 To hold temperature rise to acceptable levels, there is a need for a rapid decline in fossil fuel use, and not simply a plateau followed by a gradual decline. Despite headwinds, investor engagement on climate risk remains strong There is no denying the backlash against climate action and the associated shift away from fossil fuels in some quarters, most notably across US politics and finance. Nevertheless, significant swathes of the investment community continue to take climate risk into account in both their stewardship activities with investee

remains strong There is no denying the backlash against climate action and the associated shift away from fossil fuels in some quarters, most notably across US politics and finance. Nevertheless, significant swathes of the investment community continue to take climate risk into account in both their stewardship activities with investee companies and in their screening process. Many large asset owners, most notably in Europe, still have stringent expectations in this area and are willing to shun asset managers who they deem insufficiently climate-conscious.2 In particular, those with long-dated liabilities such as pension funds cannot overlook the unavoidable impact of the energy transition on their wide-ranging investments.3 Some have simply opted to be less vocal about their climate-informed investing, a phenomenon that has been dubbed “greenhushing”. Money managers thus ignore corporates’ alignment with the Paris Agreement at their peril. A multidimensional approach is needed to assess the oil and gas sector... As companies that form part of a value chain producing vast quantities of greenhouse gas emissions, the alignment of the largest oil and gas producers is of significant interest to investors with a climate mandate. In order to provide a holistic picture of the extent to which a producer may be considered Paris-aligned, we believe multiple factors must be taken into account: 1. Investment options: the compatibility of a company’s portfolio of potential Upstream projects with different temperature scenarios. 2. Recent sanctions: the compatibility of a company’s recently approved (or “sanctioned”) projects with different temperature scenarios. 3. Production plans: the oil and gas volumes which a company is planning to produce in the short and long term. 4. Greenhouse gas emissions targets: the coverage and ambition of a company’s overall emissions reduction targets. 1 International Energy Agency, Executive Summary – World Energy Outlook 2024 – Analysis UN Environment Programme, Emissions Gap Report 2024 2 Financial Times, Top UK pension fund pulls £28bn from State Street over ESG retreat (2025), Long-term investors split with asset managers over climate risk (2025) 3 UNEP Finance Initiative, Universal Ownership – Why environmental externalities matter to institutional investors Analyst Note – www.carbontracker.org 5 Paris Maligned

Gap Report 2024 2 Financial Times, Top UK pension fund pulls £28bn from State Street over ESG retreat (2025), Long-term investors split with asset managers over climate risk (2025) 3 UNEP Finance Initiative, Universal Ownership – Why environmental externalities matter to institutional investors Analyst Note – www.carbontracker.org 5 Paris Maligned III 5. Methane emissions targets: the coverage and ambition of a company’s methane-specific reduction targets, and its progress in directly measuring all its emissions. 6. Executive remuneration: the proportion of variable pay incentivising growth in oil and gas production. …But companies’ clean energy investments should not come into play While much attention has been paid to oil and gas companies’ attempts to scale up investment in non-fossil energy, such as wind and solar power, this does not feature in our analysis. There is no reason why oil and gas producers should necessarily seek to become renewables companies, since others may well be better placed to develop and deploy clean energy cost-effectively. Moreover, increasing investment in clean energy may not lead to a decline in a producer’s overall emissions, as this is largely a function of their oil and gas production. Our assessment is strategy-agnostic, so although recent decisions by European companies to increase new hydrocarbon developments affect their score, their cutting of green investment does not.4 While some observers may find these renewables rollbacks disappointing, it is important to bear in mind that the oil and gas sector accounted for a negligible proportion of global clean energy investment.5 The findings outlined below can be used by a range of stakeholders Investors, banks and insurers with a climate mandate will find this analysis useful in their screening processes and to inform proxy voting. However, mainstream investors concerned solely with financial return will also gain insights, by viewing companies’ scores as an indicator of transition risk exposure and preparedness for the energy transition. Finally, our findings can be used to support policymakers and civil society actors in holding corporations accountable, for instance over misleading advertising. 4 BBC, Norwegian oil giant Equinor cuts green investment in half 5 IEA, Executive summary – The

indicator of transition risk exposure and preparedness for the energy transition. Finally, our findings can be used to support policymakers and civil society actors in holding corporations accountable, for instance over misleading advertising. 4 BBC, Norwegian oil giant Equinor cuts green investment in half 5 IEA, Executive summary – The Oil and Gas Industry in Net Zero Transitions Analyst Note – www.carbontracker.org 6 Paris Maligned III 4 Assessing oil and gas sector climate alignment As in last year’s Paris Maligned II, this note assesses companies against the metrics listed above on a scale from 0 to 4 to produce an overall weighted average score. From this, we assign a grade for each producer on a scale from A to H. We use our least-cost modelling approach to assess the compatibility of companies’ potential Upstream assets with oil and gas demand forecasts from the International Energy Agency’s (IEA) energy scenarios. Using asset-level supply data from Rystad Energy, we build cost curves for global oil and regional gas markets to evaluate potential cumulative volumes that could meet remaining demand after accounting for volumes already committed from sanctioned assets. Our model assumes demand is met first by lower-cost assets. As demand declines, higher breakeven assets are more likely to fail to meet hurdle rates and shut down early, before lower- cost projects (see Figure 1). For a detailed explanation of Carbon Tracker’s modelling, refer to our Oil and Gas Least-Cost Methodology. Descriptions of the IEA scenarios are provided in Box 1. FIGURE 1. Analyst Note – www.carbontracker.org 7 Paris Maligned III Our company universe consists of 30 of the largest producers, including 20 majority publicly traded or privately owned companies, 5 majority state-owned companies, and 5 wholly state- owned companies. Producers based in countries subject to international sanctions (Russia, Iran, Venezuela) are not assessed. BOX 1. SUMMARY OF IEA SCENARIOS6 Slow

Our company universe consists of 30 of the largest producers, including 20 majority publicly traded or privately owned companies, 5 majority state-owned companies, and 5 wholly state- owned companies. Producers based in countries subject to international sanctions (Russia, Iran, Venezuela) are not assessed. BOX 1. SUMMARY OF IEA SCENARIOS6 Slow transition (2.4˚C) – The Stated Policies Scenario (STEPS): forecasts future demand where existing government policies are maintained but does not take into account pledges which have only been announced. In effect, it can be considered a continuation of the status quo - many oil and gas companies are effectively planning on this pathway.7 Under this scenario, oil demand peaks before 2030, and is associated with a temperature rise of 2.4˚C in 2100. STEPS is a forecast scenario, contingent on the level of policy action. Moderate transition (1.7˚C) – The Announced Pledges Scenario (APS): forecasts a demand trajectory based on announced government targets, regardless of whether pledges are supported by the necessary policies. Under APS, renewables generate c. 50% of all electricity by 2030; oil demand peaks imminently and declines by ~6% by 2030; the emissions trajectory leads to 1.7˚C of warming by 2100. APS is a forecast scenario, contingent on level of policy action. Fast transition (1.5˚C) – The Net Zero Emissions by 2050 Scenario (NZE): sets out what is required to achieve a 1.5 ˚C by 2100 warming outcome, which includes a 60% decrease in oil and gas demand by 2040. NZE is a normative scenario, laying out a pathway for the highest ambition under the Paris Agreement. 4.1 Changes since last year’s analysis We have expanded our company universe since last year, when 25 producers were assessed, to include more national oil companies (NOCs). This year, we also added a standalone metric on methane emissions targets, as distinct from overall greenhouse gas emissions, to give greater prominence to this urgent challenge. We also expanded our company universe, with the addition of five wholly state-owned national oil companies (Qatar Energy, KPC, Sonatrach, PEMEX, Petronas), and majority state-owned ADNOC, as well as UK North Sea producer Harbour and US shale

from overall greenhouse gas emissions, to give greater prominence to this urgent challenge. We also expanded our company universe, with the addition of five wholly state-owned national oil companies (Qatar Energy, KPC, Sonatrach, PEMEX, Petronas), and majority state-owned ADNOC, as well as UK North Sea producer Harbour and US shale driller Ovintiv. Additionally, several mergers have been completed in the past year, affecting the universe’s composition. ExxonMobil completed its acquisition of Pioneer, Chesapeake acquired Southwestern to create Expand Energy, and Harbour acquired German producer Wintershall Dea’s non-Russian assets. Chevron’s acquisition of Hess is still pending. While the reasons behind this M&A activity vary, they do not necessarily have an impact on companies’ climate alignment. The net result could be positive for the climate if companies divert earnings that might otherwise have been allocated to new Upstream projects, and simply steward existing assets to natural depletion. A negative outcome would result if the acquisition led to the company gaining new project options that would not have otherwise been developed. Thus, further analysis is needed in each case to determine the climate implications. 6 IEA, World Energy Outlook 2024 7 See Carbon Tracker’s Oil and Gas Company Profiles for details on producers’ choices of demand scenario for investment decision-making. Only one company scores well on investment options – Saudi Aramco with a 3 – thanks to its comparatively low-cost production. We score based on the proportion of project capex that would be economic under a Paris-aligned 1.7˚C scenario, awarding the top score of 4 to companies that have approved no projects.10 10 It should be noted that since this is a relative measure, there will be cases where a producer may have approved only a small volume of new production but a large proportion of this is high-cost and therefore incompatible with the 1.7˚C APS scenario, leading to a low score (as with Coterra) – or vice versa (as with Petrobras). Analyst Note – www.carbontracker.org 11 Paris Maligned III We find that few new developments have been proceeding in the high-cost Canadian oil sands, with Cenovus and Suncor both receiving a 4. This may be interpreted as a tacit acknowledgement of the energy transition and a reluctance to lock in long-cycle, high-capex projects, with a greater focus on optimising existing assets. While this is also the case with some US shale producers and may indicate a lack of competitiveness as the transition progresses, it is also partly reflective of the incremental, well-by-well nature of shale development, where the distinction between a “new project” and ongoing drilling activity is less clear-cut compared to conventional long-cycle projects. The remaining companies are spread evenly across the scores, with no clear trends based on geography or producer type, beyond the finding that shale producers which have approved projects in recent years (EOG and Coterra) score poorly, reflecting high breakeven prices. The remaining companies are spread evenly across the scores, with no clear trends based on geography or producer type, beyond the finding that shale producers which have approved projects in recent years (EOG and Coterra) score poorly, reflecting high breakeven prices. We find that many companies’ scores have fallen since last year, reflecting a shift towards less competitive (and thus climate-compatible) projects, a higher volume of new projects, and the shrinking space in the carbon budget for new production. Major LNG projects are locking companies into long-term misalignment Table 3 lists some of the largest capital projects recently sanctioned that fall outside the breakeven prices for APS in our analysis, with several also misaligned with STEPS. Many of these are LNG projects, highlighted in light blue, reflecting their high capital costs and heightened risk of stranding amid an impending LNG oversupply. Their inclusion potentially pushes other high-cost conventional projects – also incompatible with STEPS – out of our top 15 examples. We explore the risks associated with LNG investments in greater detail in our recent report, Turning Tides. This assessment of recent project sanctions provides investors with a reality check on how companies are allocating capital, beyond stated ambitions. It underscores the transition risk embedded in major investment decisions and highlights the lack of climate alignment that large- scale LNG projects bring to company portfolios. 2023 oil production baseline is from comments made by the CEO in October 2023; Shell's LNG target is for sales as it does not appear to have a liquefaction target. 5.4 Greenhouse gas emissions targets This metric scores companies based on the extent of their overall greenhouse gas emissions targets (see Box 2). Eni scores highest, with a 3, as a result of having an emissions target that meets all three of our “Hallmarks” that relate to overall GHGs. It misses out on a 4 for relying on emissions reduction strategies of questionable credibility, such as asset divestments; carbon capture, utilisation and storage (CCUS); and nature-based solutions (NBS). Several European and North American listed companies are awarded 2s and 1s for meeting some but not all of the Hallmarks – and rely on similarly questionable emissions reduction strategies as Eni, to varying degrees. The majority of companies, however, score only a 0 for this metric, including all of the newly added companies this year: both NOCs and listed US and UK producers Ovintiv and Harbour. This reflects the continued lack of targets covering scope 3 emissions: these are produced at the point of use and account for the vast majority of lifecycle oil and gas emissions. No changes to scoring have occurred since last year for companies that were assessed. Financial institutions committed to climate goals or Paris alignment must assess whether to engage with or continue financing companies that remain misaligned, especially those failing to strengthen their emissions targets over time. Analyst Note – www.carbontracker.org 16 Paris Maligned III Box 2. Hallmarks of Paris-aligned Emissions Targets Oil and gas company emissions targets should satisfy the following four Hallmarks, at a minimum, to potentially be considered Paris-aligned: 1. Cover full lifecycle GHG emissions, including scope 1, 2, and end-use 3 emissions 2. Target net-zero GHG emissions by 2050, with interim absolute reductions, across full lifecycle emissions 3. Cover GHG emissions from production and global product sales on a full equity share basis (including Downstream product sales produced using third-party crude) 4. Target near-zero methane by 2030 on a full equity

emissions 2. Target net-zero GHG emissions by 2050, with interim absolute reductions, across full lifecycle emissions 3. Cover GHG emissions from production and global product sales on a full equity share basis (including Downstream product sales produced using third-party crude) 4. Target near-zero methane by 2030 on a full equity share basis across Upstream activities, as well as Midstream gas assets where applicable 5.5 Methane emissions targets Our newly added metric on methane emissions scores companies based on the extent to which they fulfil our Hallmark 4, listed above, as well as their progress in directly measuring all of their emissions. The latter is assessed by the UN-backed Oil and Gas Methane Partnership (OGMP) 2.0, which seeks to address the sector-wide underestimating of methane emissions caused by reliance on generic “emission factors” – a topic we discuss in more detail in Absolute Impact 2024. Companies typically score higher on methane than overall GHGs, gaining more 2s and fewer 0s. NOCs in particular perform better here, reflecting the growing momentum to reduce methane emissions to “near zero” in the near term, as promoted by initiatives such as the Oil and Gas Decarbonization Charter, launched at COP28 in Dubai. It also underscores the feasibility of addressing these operational emissions without a producer fundamentally needing to change its business model (unlike with scope 3 GHG emissions). Despite this progress, companies are still a significant way from Paris alignment for several reasons. First, because targets are almost exclusively limited to operated assets, overlooking the significant proportion of production stemming from non-operated assets. Second, some companies overlook their Midstream gas assets, despite the contribution these make to the sector’s methane emissions. Third, most companies are not meeting the “Gold Standard” requirements under OGMP 2.0 and are thus likely to be underestimating their methane footprint. 5.6 Executive remuneration The scores for executive remuneration have remained broadly similar to last year, with companies continuing to reward production growth through direct or indirect growth incentives included in executives’ variable pay. Occidental is the only company to score a 3 as a result of having no growth incentives, but

Executive remuneration The scores for executive remuneration have remained broadly similar to last year, with companies continuing to reward production growth through direct or indirect growth incentives included in executives’ variable pay. Occidental is the only company to score a 3 as a result of having no growth incentives, but misses out on a 4 through its lack of incentive for emissions reductions in line with its target. Four European and North American producers receive a 2, with the remainder of companies receiving either a 1 or zero. All NOCs score a zero because of not disclosing the composition of their executive remuneration. See Crude Intentions III. 5.7 Combined alignment Bringing together the results from all six metrics, our analysis finds all producers assessed remain far from Paris-aligned, with many moving in the wrong direction (Table 4). None receive higher than a D grade and none receive high scores (a 3 or 4) for more than one of the six metrics. While some scores have improved in the past year, overall there has been backsliding, particularly around oil and gas production plans (see Section 5.3). Spanish-listed producer Repsol takes the top spot this year, kicking UK-headquartered major bp off its pedestal, following the latter’s decision to abandon its target to curb production. Four companies share the last position: US major ConocoPhillips, alongside three newly added national oil companies (NOCs) – KPC, PEMEX, and Sonatrach. Europeans lead the pack, with Americans and NOCs trailing behind Listed companies based in Europe fare best, securing seven of the top 10 places. US giant ExxonMobil sits near the bottom alongside ConocoPhillips as the two worst-performing investor- owned producers. All of the six NOCs we added to our company universe this year performed poorly, almost exclusively scoring zero for three of our metrics: production plans, greenhouse gas emission targets and executive

10 places. US giant ExxonMobil sits near the bottom alongside ConocoPhillips as the two worst-performing investor- owned producers. All of the six NOCs we added to our company universe this year performed poorly, almost exclusively scoring zero for three of our metrics: production plans, greenhouse gas emission targets and executive remuneration. This follows a broader trend among NOCs in general, though some exceptions exist: namely Norwegian majority state-owned producer Equinor, and to a lesser extent Saudi Aramco. Two further additions to our universe produce contrasting results: UK-based Harbour does comparatively well while US shale driller Ovintiv sits towards the bottom of the table. Companies exhibit a mix of strengths and weaknesses While several producers score well on one particular metric, they typically perform poorly on the others. This is the case for producers that do not appear to have approved any new projects recently, such as Suncor and Devon, although, in the case of shale producers, this may in part be a function of our ability to distinguish standalone “new projects” from the well-by-well nature of shale developments. Other examples include Occidental, which has the highest score on remuneration, thanks to its lack of production growth metrics, and PEMEX, which does comparatively well on production plans but receives zeroes for the remaining metrics. Others include their exploration plans, stated capex plans, lobbying activities, production lifetime of assets, and asset sales (see Box 3). BOX 3. OTHER FACTORS RELEVANT FOR ASSESSING PARIS ALIGNMENT • Exploration plans: Given the IEA’s stipulation that virtually no new oil and gas fields are possible in a 1.5˚C scenario, investors seeking 1.5˚C alignment should scrutinise company plans to continue frontier exploration12 which could be viewed as an indication that a company intends to expand production. • Capex on existing vs new projects: the ratio between investment on new and existing projects can give insight into a company’s production and development strategy, and the extent to which it intends to pursue new projects. • Lobbying: lobbying efforts by companies and their industry alliances and associates could undermine climate-related regulations and policies. Investors should engage with companies on how it justifies lobbying activities that could hinder the achievement of Paris goals.13 • Lifetime of assets: companies with a higher proportion of short-cycle (shale) assets enjoy more flexibility and may result in less oil and gas infrastructure lock-in. However, this must be weighed against operational emissions, principally methane, given the high methane footprint of onshore production in the US. • Asset sales: companies’ decisions on whether to divest assets, and to whom, can have a significant effect on emissions, which will rise if an asset is

infrastructure lock-in. However, this must be weighed against operational emissions, principally methane, given the high methane footprint of onshore production in the US. • Asset sales: companies’ decisions on whether to divest assets, and to whom, can have a significant effect on emissions, which will rise if an asset is sold to a less responsible operator. This topic is covered in our Responsible Exit Principles for Oil and Gas Companies report. 5.8 Considerations for Stakeholders The findings in this report provide a framework for investors, financial institutions, policymakers, and civil society to assess oil and gas producers' real-world alignment with climate goals. While corporate pledges and climate strategies are widely publicised, true alignment is demonstrated through the metrics we assess, including capital allocation, production plans, and executive incentives. Below we outline how different stakeholders can use this analysis to drive accountability, mitigate risk, and push for meaningful change. A) Responsible investment and stewardship teams: to inform asset allocation decisions, particularly within “sustainable” financial products, and to inform proxy voting a. European oil and gas companies -- BP, Shell, TotalEnergies -- backtrack on climate commitments
TotalEnergies shareholders sacrifice the climate with 2024 votes

At TotalEnergies’ 2025 annual shareholders’ meeting, despite the announced climate setbacks, shareholders continued to support the fossil fuel expansion strategy of its executives. Except for a few shareholders identified in our analysis, almost all shareholders approved their remuneration and the re-election of the only outgoing director, as well as the financial statements and dividend payments. Reclaim Finance calls on these TotalEnergies shareholders to radically change their approach and to sanction the company’s climate-damaging strategy by voting against the resolutions proposed by management.
Reclaim Finance’s analysis of the votes cast by the ten largest global, European, and French shareholders in 2025 reveals their climate irresponsibility . These shareholders had already granted an overwhelming support for the strategy of TotalEnergies’ board of directors in 2024 . They confirmed this support in 2025, even though the board had reduced its shareholders’ opportunities to express their views by refusing to organize a vote of approval for its climate plan (Say on Climate vote). This support reinforces TotalEnergies in its strategy, as illustrated by the recent political lobbying of its CEO Patrick Pouyanné against European corporate due diligence obligations .( 1)
TotalEnergies’ strategy is increasingly climate-damaging and confirms shareholder dialogue failure
The science-based recommendations are clear and unequivocal: the IPCC and the scientific community remind us that any new fossil fuel infrastructure compromises alignment with a 1.5°C carbon budget.(2) And the International Energy Agency’s (IEA) Net-Zero 2050 scenario projects a 1.5°C trajectory with no new oil and gas fields and liquefied natural gas export terminals.(3)
In contradiction with these scientific findings, TotalEnergies was once again the 6 th largest oil and gas field developer in the world in 2024,(4) and its deliberate climate-damaging strategy shows no signs of improvement. TotalEnergies recently announced a reduction in its investment forecasts for its “low-carbon” activities and an increase in the share of its investments in new oil and gas projects in 2030.(5) This comes on top of the fact that last year, TotalEnergies increased its planned oil and gas production.(6)
However, the company’s management chose not to allow shareholders to vote on its climate strategy, even though a record 20% had opposed it in 2024. In the absence of a Say on Climate resolution, and after decades of unsuccessful engagement, including through the Climate Action 100+ collaborative engagement initiative, the only responsible response would have been to take escalation measures by voting against the strategic resolutions proposed by management.
But in reality, the vast majority of shareholders continue to endorse the strategy proposed by management, voting for the re-election of outgoing directors and the compensation of the Chairman and CEO.(7) None of the top 10 global shareholders oppose these key resolutions. Among the largest French and European shareholders, only Union Investment, DEKA, and Ofi Invest AM vote against the re-election of Lise Croteau, who is yet responsible for the company’s strategy. And only Ostrum AM, La Banque Postale AM, and Ofi Invest AM vote against the CEO’s remuneration.
Although these votes were not systematically justified on climate grounds, these shareholders, who opposed strategic resolutions, had already stood out by voting against or abstaining from voting on the 2024 Say on Climate vote. Such opposition votes follow the approach recommended by academic research on investor impact.(8) These management-proposed resolutions have a binding effect on companies – unlike a Say on Climate resolution – and send the message that climate must be a non-negotiable priority.
Conversely, the votes of some shareholders in 2025 are inconsistent with those of 2024: DWS, Dimensional Fund Advisers, and UBS Asset Management voted against TotalEnergies’ climate plan in 2024, in line with science-based recommendations. In 2025, however, these same shareholders approved all resolutions. Overall, TotalEnergies shareholders voted on average in favor of 94.8% of the management-proposed resolutions,(9) thus expressing strong support for the company’s current strategy.
When the race for profit ignores climate risks
Through their votes, TotalEnergies shareholders are adopting a short-term approach that prioritizes short-term financial returns over the investments needed to transition away from fossil fuels. Almost all shareholders approved the allocation of dividends and share buybacks,(10) even though the company should use its profits to invest in sustainable energy instead. Among the main shareholders, only Union Investment voted against the share buybacks proposed by the company. As a reminder, in 2024, for every dollar invested in its electricity generation activities, which also include gas activities, TotalEnergies allocated 4 dollars to shareholders . Furthermore, while TotalEnergies’ financial statements do not adequately consider climate-related risks,(11) the vast majority of shareholders approved them at the general meeting. Among the company’s major shareholders worldwide, in Europe, and in France, only La Française – Crédit Mutuel Asset Management (La Française – Crédit Mutuel AM) refused to approve these financial statements, which partly fail to consider climate risks affecting shareholders.
TotalEnergies’ climate strategy is deteriorating, even though it was already far from aligned with scientific recommendations. The company continues to categorically refuse to consult its shareholders on its climate strategy. This confirms that shareholders must take stronger action. There is an urgent need to use greater leverage, such as voting against management’s strategic resolutions and halting new investments in TotalEnergies, particularly the purchase of its bonds.

To assess BP’s climate strategy and provide our analysis, Reclaim Finance relied on the International Energy Agen- cy’s (IEA) Net Zero Emissions by 2050 Scenario (NZE).1 The NZE is based on a 1.5°C trajectory and includes: • A drop in oil and gas production of 21,4% and 13,1% respectively by 2030, compared with 2023 levels. • A halt to the development of new oil

International Energy Agen- cy’s (IEA) Net Zero Emissions by 2050 Scenario (NZE).1 The NZE is based on a 1.5°C trajectory and includes: • A drop in oil and gas production of 21,4% and 13,1% respectively by 2030, compared with 2023 levels. • A halt to the development of new oil and gas produc- tion projects and liquefied natural gas (LNG) terminals. • A 67% increase in total annual investment in energy, with a 2.3-fold increase in annual investment in ener- gy transition, covering clean energy supply, end-use and energy efficiency. This would mean investing ten dollars in the transition by 2030, six in energy supply – mainly electricity – for every dollar invested in fossil fuels, i.e. a 6:1 ratio. BP ranks as the 12th biggest oil and gas producer and the 15th biggest oil and gas exploration and production deve- loper worldwide.2 As one of the top European integrated oil and gas compa- nies and one of the largest greenhouse gas (GHG) emit- ters globally, BP is among the few companies in the world whose climate transition (or lack thereof) in the coming years will have a determining impact on our collective abi- lity to limit global temperature rise to 1.5°C. In 2020, the company pledged to achieve carbon neutrality across its entire operations on an absolute basis by 2050 or soo- ner.3 In 2025, BP reset its strategy, keeping their net zero commitment on sales and on its scope 1 and 2 operations, but retiring its scope 3 net zero production target, and backtracking on many commitments.4 7 NOITCUDORTNI 1. The major abandoned all its scope 3 production emission targets, while scope 3 emissions are by far the largest, representing 91% of the company’s 2024 emissions. BP also reduced its scope 1 and 2 2030 targets, previously planning a 50% reduction, the company now plans a reduction comprised between 45% and 50%. The average lifecycle carbon intensity target was also strongly reduced, from -15%/-20% previously planned to -8%/-10% planned now. EMISSIONS TARGETS 32 While a growing number of financial institutions are disengaging from the oil and gas sector, deeming it in- capable of transformation, others believe that oil and gas companies are essential to the energy transition and that their support is indispensable to the massive development of renewable energies. While Shell’s in- vestment strategy already heavily relied on hydro- carbons, especially on integrated gas and LNG, the company backtracked on key climate commitments, including on its oil and gas production and investments in renewable energy. To assess Shell’s climate strategy and provide our ana- lysis, Reclaim Finance relied on the International Ener- gy Agency’s (IEA) Net Zero Emissions by 2050 Scena- rio (NZE).1 The NZE is based on a 1.5°C trajectory and includes: • A drop in oil and gas production of 21.4% and 13.1%

To assess Shell’s climate strategy and provide our ana- lysis, Reclaim Finance relied on the International Ener- gy Agency’s (IEA) Net Zero Emissions by 2050 Scena- rio (NZE).1 The NZE is based on a 1.5°C trajectory and includes: • A drop in oil and gas production of 21.4% and 13.1% respectively by 2030, compared with 2023 levels. • A halt to the development of new oil and gas pro- duction projects and liquefied natural gas (LNG) terminals. • A 3-fold increase in installed renewables capacity by 2030, which requires doubling current invest- ment levels in renewable power, grids and battery storage to US$2.5 trillion by 2030. Shell ranks as the 10th biggest oil and gas producer and the 10th biggest oil and gas upstream developer worldwide. The company is the 9th largest LNG export terminal developer.2 As one of the top European integrated oil and gas com- panies and one of the largest greenhouse gas (GHG) emitters globally, Shell is among the few companies in the world whose climate transition (or lack thereof) in the coming years will have a determining impact on our collective ability to limit global temperature rise to 1.5°C. In 2020, the company pledged to achieve car- bon neutrality across its entire operations on an abso- lute basis by 2050 or sooner.3 7 NOITCUDORTNI 1. SHELL’S INVESTMENT STRATEGY PRIORITIZES THE OIL AND GAS SECTOR AND REDISTRIBUTION TO SHAREHOLDERS, TO THE DETRIMENT OF CLIMATE SOLUTIONS • Shell invested in oil and gas rather than in renewable energy:4 For every dol- lar invested in 2024 in its “Renewables and energy solutions” business – inclu- ding renewable energy, hydrogen, Car- bon Capture and Storage (CCS) and Na- ture-Based Solutions – Shell invested 7.2 dollars in oil and gas. • Shell remunerates shareholders rather than investing in renewable energy: For every dollar invested in 2024 in its “Renewables and energy solutions” bu- siness, Shell distributed 9.0 dollars to its shareholders through dividends and share buybacks. • Shell plans to allocate only 9% of its fu- ture capital expenditure to “Renewables and energy solutions” from 2025 to 2030. 9 SGNIDNIF YEK

It was founded in 2020 and is 100% dedi- cated to issues linking finance with social and climate justice. In the context of the climate emergency and bio- diversity losses, one of Reclaim Finance’s priorities is to accelerate the decarbonization of financial flows. Reclaim Finance exposes the climate impacts of financial players, denounces the most harmful practices and puts its ex- pertise at the service of public authorities and financial stakeholders who desire to bend existing practices to ecological imperatives. contact@reclaimfinance.org

Level 0: Unaware of Climate Change as a Business Issue
1. Does the company acknowledge climate change as a significant issue for the business?
Level 1: Acknowledging Climate Change as a Business Issue
2. Does the company recognise climate change as a relevant risk and/or opportunity for the business?
3. Does the company have a policy (or equivalent) commitment to action on climate change?
4. Has the company set greenhouse gas emission reduction targets?
5. Has the company published information on its Scope 1 and 2 greenhouse gas emissions?
Level 3: Integrating into Operational Decision Making
6. Has the company nominated a board member or board committee with explicit responsibility for oversight of the climate change policy?
7. Has the company set quantitative targets for reducing its greenhouse gas emissions?
8. Does the company report on Scope 3 emissions?
9. Has the company had its operational (Scope 1 and/or 2) greenhouse gas emissions data verified?
10. Does the company support domestic and international efforts to mitigate climate change?
11. Does the company have a process to manage climate-related risks?
12. Does the company disclose materially important Scope 3 emissions?
13. Has the company set long-term quantitative targets for reducing its greenhouse gas emissions?
14. Does the company's remuneration for senior executives incorporate climate change performance?
15. Does the company incorporate climate change risks and opportunities in their strategy?
16. Does the company undertake climate scenario planning?
17. Does the company disclose an internal price of carbon?
18. Does the company disclose the actions necessary to meet its emissions-reduction targets?
Level 5: Transition Planning and Implementation
19. Does the company quantify the key elements of its emissions reduction strategy and the proportional impact of each action in achieving its targets?
20. Does the company's transition plan clarify the role that will be played by offsets and/or negative emissions technologies?
21. Does the company commit to phasing out capital expenditure on carbon intensive assets or products?
22. Does the company align future capital expenditures with its long-term decarbonisation goals and disclose how the alignment is determined?
23. Does the company ensure consistency between its climate change policy and the positions taken by trade associations of which it is a member?

14. Does the company's remuneration for senior executives incorporate climate change performance?
15. Does the company incorporate climate change risks and opportunities in their strategy?
16. Does the company undertake climate scenario planning?
17. Does the company disclose an internal price of carbon?
18. Does the company disclose the actions necessary to meet its emissions-reduction targets?
Level 5: Transition Planning and Implementation
19. Does the company quantify the key elements of its emissions reduction strategy and the proportional impact of each action in achieving its targets?
20. Does the company's transition plan clarify the role that will be played by offsets and/or negative emissions technologies?
21. Does the company commit to phasing out capital expenditure on carbon intensive assets or products?
22. Does the company align future capital expenditures with its long-term decarbonisation goals and disclose how the alignment is determined?
23. Does the company ensure consistency between its climate change policy and the positions taken by trade associations of which it is a member?
